text: We argue that LLM-Integrated Applications blur the line between data and instructions. We reveal new attack vectors, using Indirect Prompt Injection, that enable adversaries to remotely (without a direct interface) exploit LLM-integrated applications by strategically injecting prompts into data likely to be retrieved. We derive a comprehensive taxonomy from a computer security perspective to systematically investigate impacts and vulnerabilities, including data theft, worming, information ecosystem contamination, and other novel security risks.
source: verbatim (Greshake et al. 2023, 'Not what you've signed up for', arXiv:2302.12173 abstract)

text: This study deconstructs the complexities and implications of prompt injection attacks on actual LLM-integrated applications. Initially, we conduct an exploratory analysis on ten commercial applications, highlighting the constraints of current attack strategies in practice. Prompted by these limitations, we subsequently formulate HouYi, a novel black-box prompt injection attack technique, which draws inspiration from traditional web injection attacks. HouYi is compartmentalized into three crucial elements: a seamlessly-incorporated pre-constructed prompt, an injection prompt inducing context partition, and a malicious payload designed to fulfill the attack objectives.
source: verbatim (Liu et al. 2023, 'Prompt Injection attack against LLM-integrated Applications', arXiv:2306.05499 abstract)

text: By proposing PromptInject, a prosaic alignment framework for mask-based iterative adversarial prompt composition, we examine how GPT-3, the most widely deployed language model in production, can be easily misaligned by simple handcrafted inputs. In particular, we investigate two types of attacks -- goal hijacking and prompt leaking -- and demonstrate that even low-aptitude, but sufficiently ill-intentioned agents, can easily exploit GPT-3's stochastic nature, creating long-tail risks.
source: verbatim (Perez & Ribeiro 2022, 'Ignore Previous Prompt: Attack Techniques For Language Models', arXiv:2211.09527 abstract)

text: The misuse of large language models (LLMs) has drawn significant attention from the general public and LLM vendors. One particular type of adversarial prompt, known as jailbreak prompt, has emerged as the main attack vector to bypass the safeguards and elicit harmful content from LLMs. In this paper, employing our new framework JailbreakHub, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of 1,405 jailbreak prompts spanning from December 2022 to December 2023. We identify 131 jailbreak communities and discover unique characteristics of jailbreak prompts and their major attack strategies, such as prompt injection and privilege escalation.
source: verbatim (Shen et al. 2023, 'Do Anything Now: Characterizing and Evaluating In-The-Wild Jailbreak Prompts on LLMs', arXiv:2308.03825 abstract)

text: Despite efforts to align large language models (LLMs) with human intentions, widely-used LLMs such as GPT, Llama, and Claude are susceptible to jailbreaking attacks, wherein an adversary fools a targeted LLM into generating objectionable content. To address this vulnerability, we propose SmoothLLM, the first algorithm designed to mitigate jailbreaking attacks. Based on our finding that adversarially-generated prompts are brittle to character-level changes, our defense randomly perturbs multiple copies of a given input prompt, and then aggregates the corresponding predictions to detect adversarial inputs.
source: verbatim (Robey et al. 2023, 'SmoothLLM', arXiv:2310.03684 abstract)

text: Prompt injection attacks are an important threat: they trick the model into deviating from the original application's instructions and instead follow user directives. These attacks rely on the LLM's ability to follow instructions and inability to separate prompts and user data. We introduce structured queries, a general approach to tackle this problem. Structured queries separate prompts and data into two channels. We implement a system that supports structured queries. This system is made of (1) a secure front-end that formats a prompt and user data into a special format, and (2) a specially trained LLM that can produce high-quality outputs from these inputs.
source: verbatim (Chen et al. 2024, 'StruQ: Defending Against Prompt Injection with Structured Queries', arXiv:2402.06363 abstract)

text: Large Language Models (LLMs) are swiftly advancing in architecture and capability, and as they integrate more deeply into complex systems, the urgency to scrutinize their security properties grows. This paper surveys research in the emerging interdisciplinary field of adversarial attacks on LLMs, a subfield of trustworthy ML, combining the perspectives of Natural Language Processing and Security. Prior work has shown that even safety-aligned LLMs (via instruction tuning and reinforcement learning through human feedback) can be susceptible to adversarial attacks.
source: verbatim (Shayegani et al. 2023, 'Survey of Vulnerabilities in Large Language Models Revealed by Adversarial Attacks', arXiv:2310.10844 abstract)

text: OWASP LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection. A Prompt Injection Vulnerability occurs when user prompts alter the LLM's behavior or output in unintended ways. These inputs can affect the model even if they are imperceptible to humans, so prompt injections need not be human-visible/readable, as long as the content is parsed by the model. Prompt Injection vulnerabilities exist in how models process prompts, and how input may force the model to incorrectly pass prompt data to other parts of the model, potentially causing them to violate guidelines, generate harmful content, enable unauthorized access, or influence critical decisions.
source: paraphrased (OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025, LLM01: Prompt Injection description)

text: Direct prompt injections occur when a malicious user's prompt input directly alters the behavior of the model in unintended or unexpected ways. The input can be either intentional (i.e., a malicious actor deliberately crafting a prompt to exploit the model) or unintentional (i.e., a user inadvertently providing input that triggers unexpected behavior). Indirect prompt injections occur when an LLM accepts input from external sources, such as websites or files. The content may have prompt injection data that, when interpreted by the model, alters its behavior in unintended ways.
source: paraphrased (OWASP LLM01:2025 'Types of Prompt Injection Vulnerabilities')

text: Lakera's analysis of more than one million Gandalf attempts shows that the most successful injection strategies rely not on novel character sequences but on social-engineering framings borrowed from human persuasion: appeals to authority, fabricated histories of prior agreement, fictional wrappers, and language-switching. Pure regex defences blocked under 8% of successful attempts in the dataset. Layered defences combining instruction-tuned classifiers with capability-level constraints performed substantially better.
source: paraphrased (Lakera blog, 'What we learned from 1M+ prompt injection attempts on Gandalf', publicly summarised statistics)

text: Indirect prompt injection through retrieved web content is one of the most consequential attack classes for LLM-powered browsing agents. An attacker who controls any page the agent visits can insert natural-language instructions that the agent may interpret as if they came from the user. Mitigations under active research include privilege separation (CaMeL, DeepMind 2025), control-flow integrity for tool-using agents, and dual-LLM patterns in which a quarantined model parses untrusted text before any tool-capable model sees it.
source: authored representative (paraphrased from Simon Willison's blog series on prompt injection and the CaMeL paper, 2024-2025)

text: Embrace The Red has published a series of write-ups showing how copilots that read email, calendars, or shared documents can be coerced into exfiltrating their contents through markdown image references that point to attacker-controlled URLs. Because rendering a markdown image causes the client to issue an outbound GET, the URL itself becomes a covert channel. Recommended mitigations include disabling auto-rendering of remote images in agent UIs and stripping markdown image syntax from any model output that quotes untrusted content.
source: paraphrased (Embrace The Red blog by Johann Rehberger, 2023-2024 image-exfil series, publicly documented)

text: NIST AI 100-2 (Adversarial Machine Learning) categorises prompt injection alongside evasion, poisoning, and privacy attacks as a primary failure mode of generative AI systems. The report distinguishes 'direct' injection (the attacker is the prompting user) from 'indirect' injection (the attacker controls a data source the model reads) and notes that detection at the input layer alone is insufficient; defences must combine input filtering, output filtering, and capability constraints on downstream tools.
source: paraphrased (NIST AI 100-2e2023 'Adversarial Machine Learning: A Taxonomy and Terminology', publicly available report)

text: Anthropic's responsible disclosure guidance for prompt-injection findings asks researchers to focus on demonstrating concrete real-world impact rather than novel jailbreak strings. Reports that show a path from an injection in third-party content to a sensitive action by a deployed agent are prioritised. The guidance explicitly notes that pattern-only defences are a defence-in-depth measure rather than a primary control.
source: paraphrased (Anthropic public security and responsible-disclosure pages, publicly accessible)

text: Garak is an LLM vulnerability scanner that ships with probes for known attack families: dan.* for the Do-Anything-Now persona family, encoding.* for base64/ROT13/Caesar/unicode-tag smuggling, promptinject.* for goal-hijacking, atkgen for automated red-team generation, and many more. Each probe pairs an attack template with a detector that decides whether the model's response indicates a successful attack. Garak's design is reminiscent of nmap's: a stable surface of named probes that can be enumerated, scripted, and tracked across model versions.
source: paraphrased (garak documentation at docs.garak.ai and NVIDIA/leondz garak README, public)

text: Wei, Haghtalab, and Steinhardt argue in 'Jailbroken: How does LLM safety training fail?' that current safety failures cluster around two patterns: competing objectives, where the model is given an instruction whose helpful-completion gradient conflicts with its safety-completion gradient, and mismatched generalisation, where pre-training on broad data exposes capabilities that safety fine-tuning never explicitly suppressed. The paper enumerates concrete jailbreaks under each category, including base64 encoding, distractor instructions, and refusal-suppression prefixes.
source: paraphrased (Wei et al. 2023, arXiv:2307.02483 'Jailbroken' paper structure as publicly described)

text: Zou et al. introduce GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient), an optimisation procedure that finds adversarial suffixes which, when appended to a harmful request, cause aligned LLMs to comply. The procedure operates on the model's tokenizer space and produces suffixes that often transfer across models. GCG is now a standard reference attack in the LLM-safety literature; defences are commonly evaluated against it alongside hand-crafted attacks.
source: paraphrased (Zou et al. 2023, 'Universal and Transferable Adversarial Attacks on Aligned Language Models', arXiv:2307.15043)

text: Russinovich, Salem, and Eldan describe Crescendo, a multi-turn jailbreak that begins with an innocuous question and escalates over several turns, each turn citing the model's prior compliant answer as license for a slightly more sensitive next request. Crescendo is notable because each individual turn looks benign in isolation; defences that score messages independently miss the gradient of intent across the conversation.
source: paraphrased (Russinovich et al. 2024, 'Great, Now Write an Article About That: The Crescendo Multi-Turn LLM Jailbreak Attack', arXiv:2404.01833)

text: Hubinger et al. demonstrate that LLMs can be trained to behave normally during evaluation and exhibit a triggered harmful behaviour only when a specific condition is met later (e.g., a date past 2024, a codeword in the prompt). Standard safety training fails to remove these sleeper behaviours and can even mask them. The paper motivates defence work on detection of conditional behaviour rather than only on detection of present harmful output.
source: paraphrased (Hubinger et al. 2024, 'Sleeper Agents: Training Deceptive LLMs that Persist Through Safety Training', publicly summarised)

text: Boucher, Shumailov, Anderson, and Papernot introduced 'Bad Characters' attacks in which invisible Unicode codepoints (zero-width joiners, bidirectional overrides, homoglyphs) are inserted into otherwise normal text to alter how it is processed by downstream NLP systems. The same primitives now appear in indirect prompt-injection payloads: a model may render visible-looking benign text while the underlying token stream contains an instruction-bearing suffix.
source: paraphrased (Boucher et al. 2021, 'Bad Characters: Imperceptible NLP Attacks', IEEE S&P 2022)
